text: Robotic transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair. Our patient is a sixty seven year old female with a one year history of progressively increasing abdominal pain and postprandial bloating. Past medical history was significant for hypertension and obesity and past surgical history was significant for open appendectomy in the remote past. She localized her pain to the upper abdomen and therefore had an extensive workup including ultrasound, EGD, and HIDA scan, which all proved negative. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis revealed a large diaphragmatic hernia containing colon. Coronal cuts further reveal transverse colon, nearly obstructing within the morgagni defect.

We utilize the tap approach for many of our hernia repairs. The ability to effectively dissect individual layers of the abdominal wall utilizing the robotic modality allows us to sandwich the mesh in between body layers. Consequently, the mesh is allowed to incorporate on both of its faces and therefore eliminate the need for trans-fascial suture fixation and minimal tact fixation. We believe this results in decreased post-operative pain and this becomes more important when repairing defects high on the abdominal wall, including the diaphragm. Another advantage of placing mesh in a sub-lay position is that it is protected from the abdominal visceral content and allows for placement of an uncoated mesh, which is distinct to that of standard conventional lab ventral hernia repair.

The surgeon performs the hernia repair using a three-port technique, with a 12mm trocar in the supraumbilical space and two 8mm robotic trocars in the left and right upper quadrants. The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg, and the robot is docked over the right shoulder. The hernia contents are reduced, followed by dissection of a preperitoneal plane caudal to the hernia defect.

The surgeon approaches the hernia sac, with the xiphoid process visible in the image. The hernia sac is reduced, revealing that the defect is larger than initially appeared. The sac is transected, and the reduction of preperitoneal fat continues.

Following the complete reduction of the hernia sac, the surgeon performs a wide dissection of the preperitoneal flap, preparing the area for mesh placement. A large 15x15 sheet of Prolene soft mesh is cut down and positioned to reinforce the repair. O-ethabon sutures are used in an interrupted fashion to close the defect, with sutures placed on the xiphoid process, diaphragm, and periosteum of the ribs. The mesh is fixated at cardinal points using a secure strap, and partial thickness sutures are applied along the diaphragm.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a secure strap, while the hernia sac is positioned to cover any exposed areas of mesh.

Patient was discharged on post-op day one, and on short-term follow-up, she has experienced complete resolution of her preoperative symptoms. Narcotics were discontinued on post-op day one, and she returned to work on post-op day four. Among our growing cohort of tap abdominal wall hernia repairs, this is a pattern that is consistent with decreased narcotic use and earlier resumption of work. We believe this may reflect overall decreased postoperative pain in comparison to our lap ventral cohorts.
